hard_spots.problem: msgspec.Struct used for cross-process serialization but never explained what msgspec is or why chosen. Ch7 covers IPC later, so reader cannot judge if this is scaffolding detail or critical mechanism.
Code deletes n>1 parallel sampling branch (ParentRequest) without explaining whether the fan-out logic is identical to main path or fundamentally different. Reader doesn't know what they're missing.

Claims continuous batching depends on three-stage pipelining to keep GPU saturated, but doesn't explain the causal mechanism. Does it mean feeding more requests faster, or processing more existing requests in parallel?

FINAL mode claims new output is old output's superset (full prefix), so override is safe. But where is this invariant enforced in RequestOutput contract? No backward reference to where it's guaranteed.
Formula T_tok2cli ≈ T_put + T_resume claims O(1) but doesn't decompose T_resume. Does it include Event.set() + scheduler overhead + coroutine context switch? No breakdown.
process_outputs branches on 'req_state.queue is not None' to handle AsyncLLM vs LLMEngine. But LLMEngine never mentioned in this chapter; feels like scaffolding leakage from synchronous code path.

Claims merge is better than backpressure because producer never blocks. But if producer runs ahead of slow consumer on same GPU, doesn't GPU output back up anyway? Why does producer not blocking help throughput?

13-step complete flow is text-only, completely depends on diagram fig-producer-consumer-seq.png (line 695). Without visual, reader cannot see which steps are concurrent vs serial, when wake-ups occur.

Claims token-to-client latency is O(1) regardless of concurrency. But doesn't address: what if put() itself contends for queue lock? What if Event.set() with N waiters scales poorly? Glosses over implementation costs.
Code gate 'if out is not STREAM_FINISHED' exists but marked orthogonal special case. Reader sees the branch in real code but gets no clue when it triggers or how to handle it.

diagram_suggestions.what: State machine diagram for (self.output, ready) pair: 5 states with put/get/get_nowait transitions, showing when Event.set() fires vs when get() blocks. Replaces dense table at lines 509-517 with visual state-transition flow, making fast-path vs slow-path obvious.

Timeline showing event loop task interleaving WITH chunking (output_handler lets go, new add_request runs) vs WITHOUT (output_handler monopolizes thread). Quantifies claim that chunk_size=128 limits blocking to 6.4ms.

Data flow diagram showing Request/Output fields with annotations: which come from Stage1 InputProcessor, which filled by EngineCore, which forward to Stage3. Helps reader trace data provenance and see what IPC actually carries.

Latency breakdown stack: show T_put (queue write) + T_set() (Event.set) + T_notify (wake registered waiters) + T_reschedule (coroutine context switch) = actual wall-clock time. Decompose the O(1) claim into its component costs.

notes: Chapter grounds three-stage async orchestration in real vLLM code faithfully. Design choices (per-request queues, merge vs backpressure, single IPC exit, chunked processing) are justified. Main issue: narrative oscillates between code-level detail and system-level intuition — several sections (FINAL-mode safety, producer-consumer scheduling, resumption cost, LLMEngine parallel path) need either deeper code context or higher-level sketches. Section 4.9 is text-only but relies entirely on diagram for temporal clarity. Per-request queue latency claim is sound but glosses over implementation scalability of Event.set() with many waiters.
